one_line_takeaway: A pure theory paper proving that the order of the Law-of-Robustness Lipschitz lower bound is scale-dependent -- unchanged (Omega(n^{1/d})) under global Rademacher complexity but rho- and sqrt(r/n)-dependent under local Rademacher complexity -- thereby resolving Bubeck & Sellke's open problem of connecting the law of robustness to robust generalization; it has no security threat model, no experiments, and no relevance to applied AI-security beyond the theoretical vocabulary of adversarial-radius Lipschitz robustness.
key_finding: The order of the Lipschitz-constant lower bound implied by robust generalization behaves differently depending on whether one measures complexity globally or locally: globally it stays Omega(n^{1/d}), matching prior distribution-free law-of-robustness results (Wu et al. 2023), but locally (restricting to the small-empirical-risk subclass actually reachable by a learning algorithm) the bound's dependence changes, now scaling with the perturbation radius rho and the localized concentration term sqrt(r/n) rather than n^{1/d} alone. The paper's Discussion frames this as: in the 'massive ocean' of the full function class, perturbations are imperceptible to the order of the bound, but zooming in to the region near the empirical optimum, rho and the concentration term become directly visible in the Lipschitz-constant order.
core_contribution: The paper (More, Das, Ponde, Mandal, Varadarajan, Gupta; NeurIPS 2026) proves the first explicit theoretical connection between the 'Law of Robustness' (Bubeck & Sellke's result that overparameterization/Lipschitzness lower bounds are needed for robust interpolation) and 'robust generalization' (whether low robust training error implies low robust test error), a link the original Bubeck-Sellke paper posed as an open problem. Using Rademacher-complexity machinery applied to the robust squared-loss class L_rho(F_L), the authors derive two distinct regimes: (1) a GLOBAL result (Theorem 1, using global Rademacher complexity of the loss class reduced to the clean loss class via an extremal-envelope/coordinatewise-maximum argument) showing the Lipschitz lower bound retains the same order, Omega(n^{1/d}), as Wu et al.'s distribution-free bound; and (2) a LOCAL result (Theorem 3, Corollary 4/5, using local Rademacher complexity restricted to functions with small empirical risk, bounded via covering-number/metric-entropy arguments over Lipschitz function classes) showing that at the local scale the order of the Lipschitz lower bound changes, becoming dependent on the perturbation radius rho and a localized concentration term sqrt(r/n). This answers the titular question 'does order matter?' with 'it depends on how closely one looks': the answer differs between the global (loss-class-wide) and local (near-optimal-hypothesis) regimes.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a pure statistical learning theory paper with no adversary, attack, or defense evaluation; 'robustness' here refers to Lipschitz-constrained interpolation and adversarial-radius perturbation in the standard learning-theoretic sense (sup over an L2 ball of radius rho around each input), used purely as a mathematical perturbation model for bounding generalization gaps, not as an operational security threat model.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors in the Discussion and the NeurIPS checklist: (1) no experimental validation of the significance of the local concentration term sqrt(r/n) -- purely theoretical; (2) results are derived for squared loss, whereas robust generalization in practice is usually studied for classification with cross-entropy loss, so extension to Bregman-divergence losses (as in Das et al. 2025) is left as future work; (3) the localized Rademacher argument is geometric and depends on n, d, D, r, rho, sigma, epsilon, delta but NOT directly on network parameter count p -- a parameter-count dependence would require an additional distributional assumption such as isoperimetry (as in Bubeck & Sellke); (4) distributional robustness as an alternative route to connect the two notions is flagged as unexplored.

quantitative_results: Global setting: Lipschitz lower bound order remains L = Omega(n^{1/d}), consistent with the distribution-free bound of Wu et al. (2023)

Local setting: L = Omega(Delta_{n,r,delta} / (rho*sqrt(sigma^2-epsilon) + D*n^{-1/d} + rho*sqrt(Delta_{n,r,delta}))), where Delta_{n,r,delta} = epsilon - 2*Cd*n^{-1/d} - 2*Cd*sqrt(r/n) - 4*sqrt(log(1/delta)/(2n))
Special case rho=0, D>0: L = Omega(Delta_{n,r,delta} * n^{1/d} / D)
Metric entropy bound: log N(eta, F_L(X), ||.||_infty) <= c_d * ((1+LD)/eta)^d (Theorem 2)
Local Rademacher upper bound for robust loss class: R_n(L_rho(r)) <= C_d * ((1+LD)*n^{-1/d} + sqrt(r/n)) for d>=3 (Theorem 3)
